title: Time-Series Database (Generic)
description: Generic base pattern for time-series databases optimized for temporal data. Stores timestamped data points with high write throughput and efficient time-range queries. Best for: IoT telemetry, metrics monitoring, financial tick data, sensor data. Common implementations: TimescaleDB, InfluxDB, Timestream. Choose a specific variant for deployment.

provides.description: Store and query time-stamped data efficiently
provides.reasoning: Core capability of time-series databases. This pattern specializes in storing timestamped data points optimized for temporal data access patterns.

provides.description: Automatic data aggregation for long-term storage
provides.reasoning: Essential feature of time-series databases to reduce data resolution over time. Allows aggregating high-frequency data into lower-frequency summaries for efficient long-term storage and querying.

Distinguishing feature of time-series databases enabling efficient queries across temporal ranges. Optimized for retrieving data within specific time windows which is fundamental to temporal data analysis.
Key characteristic explicitly mentioned in the pattern description. Time-series databases are optimized to handle continuous high-volume writes from IoT devices, sensors, and monitoring systems.

Common time-series database feature for automatic data expiration and lifecycle management. Enables efficient storage management by automatically removing old data based on configurable retention rules.
Core capability for time-series analytics enabling aggregation operations over time windows. Essential for computing metrics like averages, sums, and percentiles across temporal dimensions.

requires.reasoning: Time-series databases need underlying persistent storage to durably store temporal data. Whether using local disks, network-attached storage, or cloud block storage, this is a fundamental requirement for any database pattern.

Requires computational resources to handle high write throughput, time-range queries, aggregations, and downsampling operations. Time-series workloads can be CPU and memory intensive, especially for real-time analytics.
Required for distributed or clustered deployments common in TimescaleDB and InfluxDB implementations. Also needed for client connectivity and data ingestion from distributed sources.

retention_days.description: Data retention period before automatic deletion. Shorter retention reduces storage costs but limits historical analysis.

downsampling_enabled.description: Enable automatic data aggregation over time (e.g., raw → hourly → daily). Reduces storage and improves query performance for older data at the cost of granularity.

compression_enabled.description: Enable time-series compression algorithms to reduce storage costs by 10-20x. Minimal query performance impact for most workloads.

write_consistency.description: Strong consistency ensures immediate read-after-write visibility but reduces write throughput. Eventual consistency optimizes for high-volume ingestion (IoT, metrics).

query_optimization.description: Optimize for recent data queries (dashboards, real-time alerts), historical analysis (reports, ML training), or balanced workloads. Affects indexing and caching strategies.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Generic time-series database costs vary by implementation. Min $0 represents free tiers (InfluxDB Cloud free tier with 30-day retention, TimescaleDB self-hosted on existing infrastructure, or AWS Timestream free tier up to 1GB). Max $2000 represents production scenarios: managed TimescaleDB (~$300-800 for 4-8 vCPU instances), InfluxDB Cloud dedicated cluster ($500-1500 depending on throughput/retention), or AWS Timestream with ~50M writes/day and 100GB storage ($800-1200). Mid-range $200-500 covers small production workloads with managed services or moderate self-hosted infrastructure (EC2 t3.large ~$60/month + EBS storage ~$100-200). Costs scale primarily with data ingestion rate, retention period, and query volume.
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-high complexity implementation. Requires understanding time-series data modeling (tags vs fields, cardinality considerations, retention policies), schema design optimized for temporal queries, setting up continuous aggregates/materialized views for performance, implementing data retention/downsampling strategies, and integration with existing data pipelines. Development effort includes: data ingestion pipeline setup (2-3 days), schema design and optimization (2-3 days), query patterns and indexing strategies (1-2 days), monitoring/alerting setup (1-2 days), and testing time-range queries at scale (1-2 days). Total 7-12 days at blended rate of ~$150-200/day with AI assistance. Learning curve moderate as time-series concepts differ from traditional RDBMS, but modern tools have good documentation.
provenance.licenseCost: Most time-series databases offer open-source core versions with no license cost: TimescaleDB (Apache 2.0/Timescale License for community edition), InfluxDB OSS (MIT), Prometheus (Apache 2.0), VictoriaMetrics (Apache 2.0). Commercial licenses apply only for enterprise features: TimescaleDB Enterprise ($0 for self-hosted open-source), InfluxDB Enterprise ($4000-20000+ annually for clustering/advanced features), AWS Timestream (pay-per-use, no license). For generic pattern assuming open-source implementations, license cost is $0. Enterprise features like advanced clustering, backup/restore tools, and premium support are optional add-ons.

supports_nfr.reason: Time-series databases with high-availability configurations can achieve up to 99.99% availability.
Time-series databases have optimized write paths and indexed time-range queries achieving p95 latency of at least 5ms.
Time-series query engines with efficient time-based indexing achieve p99 latency of at least 20ms.
Automated failover to hot standby replicas completes within 1-10 minutes depending on configuration.
Time-series databases are optimized for high write throughput handling up to 100K writes/second (360M per hour).
Time-series databases can handle up to 50K read queries per second for time-range analytical queries.
Time-series databases are optimized for high-frequency writes supporting up to 100K write operations per second.
Time-series databases support various tenant isolation strategies from row-level to database-per-tenant.
Time-series databases can store any data type including PII depending on application requirements.
GDPR compliance depends on operational procedures, data retention policies, and encryption configurations.
HIPAA compliance requires additional operational controls, encryption, and audit logging that can be configured.
CCPA compliance depends on data handling procedures and consumer rights management implemented on top of the database.
SOX compliance for financial data requires audit trails and access controls that can be implemented with time-series databases.
Time-series databases support read-your-writes consistency for single-client sessions or eventual consistency for replicas.
Time-series databases provide configurable durability through write-ahead logging and synchronous replication options.

supports_constraints.reason: Pattern specifically requires time-series database capability as its core feature and defining characteristic.

requires_constraints.reason: Generic time-series database pattern is cloud-agnostic and can be deployed on any cloud provider or on-premises.
Time-series databases are infrastructure components accessed via network protocols from any programming language.
Time-series databases support backend platforms that generate or consume temporal data streams and metrics.
Time-series databases work with or without additional caching layers depending on read access patterns.
Time-series databases support both direct writes and ingestion from async messaging systems like Kafka.
Time-series databases can store telemetry and metrics from AI inference workloads or operate independently.
Time-series databases support multi-tenant architectures through partitioning, tagging, or separate database instances.
Time-series databases support both continuous data ingestion and batch loading of historical time-series data.
Time-series databases typically optimize for append-only writes rather than distributed transactions but can coexist with transactional systems.
Time-series databases can handle both real-time streaming data ingestion and batch historical data loading.
Time-series databases focus on temporal queries but can coexist with vector search capabilities in polyglot architectures.
Time-series databases are specialized for temporal data but can store document-like metadata alongside time-series points.
Time-series databases are optimized for time-range queries rather than key-value lookups but support both access patterns.
Time-series databases focus on temporal relationships rather than graph relationships but can complement graph databases.
Time-series databases handle high-frequency transactional writes for metrics and telemetry but also support analytical queries.
Time-series databases support analytical queries over time ranges including aggregations, downsampling, and windowing functions.

requires_nfr.reason: Time-series databases with high-availability configurations can achieve up to 99.99% availability.
Time-series databases have optimized write paths and indexed time-range queries achieving p95 latency of at least 5ms.
Time-series query engines with efficient time-based indexing achieve p99 latency of at least 20ms.
Automated failover to hot standby replicas completes within 1-10 minutes depending on configuration.
Synchronous replication modes provide near-zero RPO with committed writes replicated to standby nodes.
Time-series databases are optimized for high write throughput handling up to 100K writes/second (360M per hour).
Time-series databases can handle up to 50K read queries per second for time-range analytical queries.
Time-series databases are optimized for high-frequency writes supporting up to 100K write operations per second.
